You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

[],\\n      env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\\n    });\\n    const client = new Client({ name: `bridge-pool-${name}`, version: \\\"1.0.0\\\" });\\n    await client.connect(transport);\\n\\n    const { tools } = await client.listTools();\\n    const schemas: ToolSchema[] = (tools ?? []).map((t) => ({\\n      name: t.name,\\n      description: t.description,\\n      inputSchema: t.inputSchema as Record<string, unknown>,\\n      _server: name,\\n    }));\\n\\n    const server: ConnectedServer = { name, client, transport, tools: schemas };\\n    this.servers.push(server);\\n    for (const t of schemas) {\\n      this.toolIndex.set(t.name, server);\\n    }\\n    console.log(`[mcp-pool] connected \\\"${name}\\\" — ${schemas.length} tools`);\",\"numLines\":45,\"startLine\":30,\"totalLines\":141}}"</outcome>

content.thinking:
The user is observing a primary session where someone is working on fixing an environment variable leakage issue in `mcp-pool.ts`. I can see a file read of the `mcp-pool.ts` file showing lines 30-75 (approximately).

Looking at the code, I can see the issue:
```typescript
env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,
```

This line is spreading `process.env` which means ALL environment variables from the parent process are being passed to the MCP server child processes. This is a security issue as it could leak sensitive environment variables.

However, I should wait to see what action is taken. This is just a file read - no modification has been made yet. I should wait for the actual fix to be implemented before recording an observation.

content.text: I see a file read of `mcp-pool.ts` showing the `connectOne` method where environment variables are being passed to child processes. The line `env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv }` spreads all parent process environment variables, which is the leakage issue. I'll wait for the actual modification to be made before recording the observation.

Claude's Full Response to User:
已 commit：`e60b747`。

一個附帶說明：repo 的 pre-commit hook（`[sync-skills]`）自動同步了 `default-skills/` 底下兩個 SKILL.md 的小更新並一起進了這個 commit——這是倉庫自己的 hook 行為，非我手動加入。你原本改到一半的 `docs/usage-guide.html` 保持未 staged，沒有動到。

總結這次問題：409 的根因是 bridge spawn agent 時把自己的 bot token 透過環境變數洩漏給子孫，Claude Code 官方 telegram plugin 撿到後開了第二條 getUpdates 連線把宿主打死。修正後 headless agent session 的 plugin 會自我停用，bridge 已穩定運作。若之後想收斂 `mcp-pool.ts` 的 env 外洩面，再叫我處理即可。

content.text:
<summary>
  <request>Fix environment variable leakage in mcp-pool.ts to prevent sensitive data exposure to child processes</request>
  <investigated>Examined mcp-pool.ts connectOne method where process.env is spread into child MCP server processes; traced how connectAll is invoked from direct.ts with mcpEnv configuration; searched for potential sensitive variables like TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN in config files</investigated>
  <learned>The mcp-pool.ts spreads all parent process environment variables (...process.env) when spawning MCP server child processes on line 56; this design previously caused a critical 409 conflict bug where the Telegram bot token leaked to Claude Code agent sessions, causing duplicate getUpdates connections; the bridge's headless agent plugin now self-disables to prevent this</learned>
  <completed>Previous fix (commit e60b747) resolved the Telegram 409 conflict by making headless agent sessions disable their plugins, preventing bot token leakage to spawned agents; bridge is now stable and operational</completed>
  <next_steps>The mcp-pool.ts environment variable leakage issue has been identified but not yet remediated; the primary session offered to fix it when the user is ready, which would involve filtering process.env to only pass necessary variables instead of spreading all parent environment variables</next_steps>
  <notes>The root cause analysis revealed that spreading process.env creates a security risk by exposing all parent environment variables (including secrets like TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN) to child MCP server processes; the immediate symptom was resolved but the underlying pattern in mcp-pool.ts remains a potential security issue for future consideration</notes>
</summary>

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <what_happened>Edit</what_happened>
  <occurred_at>2026-07-06T03:38:19.195Z</occurred_at>
  <working_directory>G:\AI\telegram-kiro-bridge-main</working_directory>
  <parameters>"{\"file_path\":\"G:\\\\AI\\\\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\\\\src\\\\provider\\\\mcp-pool.ts\",\"old_string\":\"      env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\",\"new_string\":\"      // TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN 不隨 process.env 外洩給 MCP 子行程（同 acpClient 的\\n      // buildSpawnEnv）；置於 cfg.env 之前——設定檔明確給值時仍可覆蓋。\\n      env: { ...process.env, TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN: \\\"\\\", ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\",\"replace_all\":false}"</parameters>
  <outcome>"{\"filePath\":\"G:\\\\AI\\\\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\\\\src\\\\provider\\\\mcp-pool.ts\",\"oldString\":\"      env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\",\"newString\":\"      // TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN 不隨 process.env 外洩給 MCP 子行程（同 acpClient 的\\n      // buildSpawnEnv）；置於 cfg.env 之前——設定檔明確給值時仍可覆蓋。\\n      env: { ...process.env, TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN: \\\"\\\", ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\",\"originalFile\":\"/**\\n * MCP Client Pool — connects to configured MCP servers (stdio transport),\\n * aggregates their tool schemas, and routes tool_use calls to the right server.\\n *\\n * Used by DirectProvider's tool loop. ACP mode doesn't need this (Kiro CLI\\n * manages its own MCP connections).\\n */\\nimport { Client } from \\\"@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/index.js\\\";\\nimport { StdioClientTransport } from \\\"@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/stdio.js\\\";\\nimport { readFile } from \\\"node:fs/promises\\\";\\nimport path from \\\"node:path\\\";\\n\\nexport interface McpServerConfig {\\n  command: string;\\n  args?: string[];\\n  env?: Record<string, string>;\\n  disabled?: boolean;\\n}\\n\\nexport interface ToolSchema {\\n  name: string;\\n  description?: string;\\n  inputSchema: Record<string, unknown>;\\n  /** Which MCP server owns this tool. */\\n  _server: string;\\n}\\n\\ninterface ConnectedServer {\\n  name: string;\\n  client: Client;\\n  transport: StdioClientTransport;\\n  tools: ToolSchema[];\\n}\\n\\nexport class McpPool {\\n  private servers: ConnectedServer[] = [];\\n  private toolIndex = new Map<string, ConnectedServer>();\\n\\n  /** Connect to all enabled MCP servers from a config object. */\\n  async connectAll(configs: Record<string, McpServerConfig>, extraEnv?: Record<string, string>): Promise<void> {\\n    const entries = Object.entries(configs).filter(([, c]) => !c.disabled);\\n    const results = await Promise.allSettled(\\n      entries.map(([name, cfg]) => this.connectOne(name, cfg, extraEnv)),\\n    );\\n    for (let i = 0; i < results.length; i++) {\\n      if (results[i].status === \\\"rejected\\\") {\\n        console.warn(`[mcp-pool] failed to connect \\\"${entries[i][0]}\\\":`, (results[i] as PromiseRejectedResult).reason?.message ?? results[i]);\\n      }\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  private async connectOne(name: string, cfg: McpServerConfig, extraEnv?: Record<string, string>): Promise<void> {\\n    const transport = new StdioClientTransport({\\n      command: cfg.command,\\n      args: cfg.args ?? [],\\n      env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\\n    });\\n    const client = new Client({ name: `bridge-pool-${name}`, version: \\\"1.0.0\\\" });\\n    await client.connect(transport);\\n\\n    const { tools } = await client.listTools();\\n    const schemas: ToolSchema[] = (tools ?? []).map((t) => ({\\n      name: t.name,\\n      description: t.description,\\n      inputSchema: t.inputSchema as Record<string, unknown>,\\n      _server: name,\\n    }));\\n\\n    const server: ConnectedServer = { name, client, transport, tools: schemas };\\n    this.servers.push(server);\\n    for (const t of schemas) {\\n      this.toolIndex.set(t.name, server);\\n    }\\n    console.log(`[mcp-pool] connected \\\"${name}\\\" — ${schemas.length} tools`);\\n  }\\n\\n  /** All tool schemas across all connected servers (for LLM API tools param). */\\n  listTools(): ToolSchema[] {\\n    return this.servers.flatMap((s) => s.tools);\\n  }\\n\\n  /** Execute a tool call, routing to the correct MCP server. */\\n  async callTool(name: string, args: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<{ content: unknown[]; isError?: boolean }> {\\n    const server = this.toolIndex.get(name);\\n    if (!server) {\\n      return { content: [{ type: \\\"text\\\", text: `Error: tool \\\"${name}\\\" not found in any MCP server` }], isError: true };\\n    }\\n    const result = await server.client.callTool({ name, arguments: args });\\n    return { content: result.content as unknown[], isError: !!result.isError };\\n  }\\n\\n  /** Gracefully close all connections. */\\n  async close(): Promise<void> {\\n    await Promise.allSettled(this.servers.map((s) => s.client.close()));\\n    this.servers = [];\\n    this.toolIndex.clear();\\n  }\\n\\n  get size(): number {\\n    return this.servers.length;\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n/**\\n * Load MCP server configs from ~/.kiro/settings/mcp.json.\\n * Also includes the bridge's own memory and Google MCP servers.\\n */\\nexport async function loadMcpConfigs(): Promise<Record<string, McpServerConfig>> {\\n  const settingsPath = path.join(\\n    process.env.AGENT_CONFIG_DIR ?? path.join(process.env.USERPROFILE ?? process.env.HOME ?? \\\"\\\", \\\".kiro\\\"),\\n    \\\"settings\\\",\\n    \\\"mcp.json\\\",\\n  );\\n  try {\\n    const raw = await readFile(settingsPath, \\\"utf8\\\");\\n    const parsed = JSON.parse(raw);\\n    const configs: Record<string, McpServerConfig> = parsed.mcpServers ?? {};\\n    // Always include the bridge's own MCP servers.\\n    if (!configs[\\\"memory\\\"]) {\\n      configs[\\\"memory\\\"] = {\\n        command: \\\"node\\\",\\n        args: [path.resolve(\\\"dist/mcp-memory.js\\\")],\\n      };\\n    }\\n    if (!configs[\\\"google\\\"]) {\\n      configs[\\\"google\\\"] = {\\n        command: \\\"node\\\",\\n        args: [path.resolve(\\\"dist/mcp-google.js\\\")],\\n      };\\n    }\\n    return configs;\\n  } catch (err) {\\n    console.warn(`[mcp-pool] could not load ${settingsPath}:`, (err as Error).message);\\n    // Fallback: bridge-owned servers only.\\n    return {\\n      memory: { command: \\\"node\\\", args: [path.resolve(\\\"dist/mcp-memory.js\\\")] },\\n      google: { command: \\\"node\\\", args: [path.resolve(\\\"dist/mcp-google.js\\\")] },\\n    };\\n  }\\n}\\n\",\"structuredPatch\":[{\"oldStart\":53,\"oldLines\":7,\"newStart\":53,\"newLines\":9,\"lines\":[\"     const transport = new StdioClientTransport({\",\"       command: cfg.command,\",\"       args: cfg.args ?? [],\",\"-      env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\",\"+      // TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN 不隨 process.env 外洩給 MCP 子行程（同 acpClient 的\",\"+      // buildSpawnEnv）；置於 cfg.env 之前——設定檔明確給值時仍可覆蓋。\",\"+      env: { ...process.env, TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN: \\\"\\\", ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\",\"     });\",\"     const client = new Client({ name: `bridge-pool-${name}`, version: \\\"1.0.0\\\" });\",\"     await client.connect(transport);\"]}],\"userModified\":false,\"replaceAll\":false}"</outcome>
</observed_from_primary_session>

ACP mode doesn't need this (Kiro CLI\\n * manages its own MCP connections).\\n */\\nimport { Client } from \\\"@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/index.js\\\";\\nimport { StdioClientTransport } from \\\"@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/stdio.js\\\";\\nimport { readFile } from \\\"node:fs/promises\\\";\\nimport path from \\\"node:path\\\";\\n\\nexport interface McpServerConfig {\\n  command: string;\\n  args?: string[];\\n  env?: Record<string, string>;\\n  disabled?: boolean;\\n}\\n\\nexport interface ToolSchema {\\n  name: string;\\n  description?: string;\\n  inputSchema: Record<string, unknown>;\\n  /** Which MCP server owns this tool. */\\n  _server: string;\\n}\\n\\ninterface ConnectedServer {\\n  name: string;\\n  client: Client;\\n  transport: StdioClientTransport;\\n  tools: ToolSchema[];\\n}\\n\\nexport class McpPool {\\n  private servers: ConnectedServer[] = [];\\n  private toolIndex = new Map<string, ConnectedServer>();\\n\\n  /** Connect to all enabled MCP servers from a config object. */\\n  async connectAll(configs: Record<string, McpServerConfig>, extraEnv?: Record<string, string>): Promise<void> {\\n    const entries = Object.entries(configs).filter(([, c]) => !c.disabled);\\n    const results = await Promise.allSettled(\\n      entries.map(([name, cfg]) => this.connectOne(name, cfg, extraEnv)),\\n    );\\n    for (let i = 0; i < results.length; i++) {\\n      if (results[i].status === \\\"rejected\\\") {\\n        console.warn(`[mcp-pool] failed to connect \\\"${entries[i][0]}\\\":`, (results[i] as PromiseRejectedResult).reason?.message ?? results[i]);\\n      }\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  private async connectOne(name: string, cfg: McpServerConfig, extraEnv?: Record<string, string>): Promise<void> {\\n    const transport = new StdioClientTransport({\\n      command: cfg.command,\\n      args: cfg.args ?? [],\\n      env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\\n    });\\n    const client = new Client({ name: `bridge-pool-${name}`, version: \\\"1.0.0\\\" });\\n    await client.connect(transport);\\n\\n    const { tools } = await client.listTools();\\n    const schemas: ToolSchema[] = (tools ?? []).map((t) => ({\\n      name: t.name,\\n      description: t.description,\\n      inputSchema: t.inputSchema as Record<string, unknown>,\\n      _server: name,\\n    }));\\n\\n    const server: ConnectedServer = { name, client, transport, tools: schemas };\\n    this.servers.push(server);\\n    for (const t of schemas) {\\n      this.toolIndex.set(t.name, server);\\n    }\\n    console.log(`[mcp-pool] connected \\\"${name}\\\" — ${schemas.length} tools`);\\n  }\\n\\n  /** All tool schemas across all connected servers (for LLM API tools param). */\\n  listTools(): ToolSchema[] {\\n    return this.servers.flatMap((s) => s.tools);\\n  }\\n\\n  /** Execute a tool call, routing to the correct MCP server. */\\n  async callTool(name: string, args: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<{ content: unknown[]; isError?: boolean }> {\\n    const server = this.toolIndex.get(name);\\n    if (!server) {\\n      return { content: [{ type: \\\"text\\\", text: `Error: tool \\\"${name}\\\" not found in any MCP server` }], isError: true };\\n    }\\n    const result = await server.client.callTool({ name, arguments: args });\\n    return { content: result.content as unknown[], isError: !!result.isError };\\n  }\\n\\n  /** Gracefully close all connections. */\\n  async close(): Promise<void> {\\n    await Promise.allSettled(this.servers.map((s) => s.client.close()));\\n    this.servers = [];\\n    this.toolIndex.clear();\\n  }\\n\\n  get size(): number {\\n    return this.servers.length;\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n/**\\n * Load MCP server configs from ~/.kiro/settings/mcp.json.\\n * Also includes the bridge's own memory and Google MCP servers.\\n */\\nexport async function loadMcpConfigs(): Promise<Record<string, McpServerConfig>> {\\n  const settingsPath = path.join(\\n    process.env.AGENT_CONFIG_DIR ?? path.join(process.env.USERPROFILE ?? process.env.HOME ?? \\\"\\\", \\\".kiro\\\"),\\n    \\\"settings\\\",\\n    \\\"mcp.json\\\",\\n  );\\n  try {\\n    const raw = await readFile(settingsPath, \\\"utf8\\\");\\n    const parsed = JSON.parse(raw);\\n    const configs: Record<string, McpServerConfig> = parsed.mcpServers ?? {};\\n    // Always include the bridge's own MCP servers.\\n    if (!configs[\\\"memory\\\"]) {\\n      configs[\\\"memory\\\"] = {\\n        command: \\\"node\\\",\\n        args: [path.resolve(\\\"dist/mcp-memory.js\\\")],\\n      };\\n    }\\n    if (!configs[\\\"google\\\"]) {\\n      configs[\\\"google\\\"] = {\\n        command: \\\"node\\\",\\n        args: [path.resolve(\\\"dist/mcp-google.js\\\")],\\n      };\\n    }\\n    return configs;\\n  } catch (err) {\\n    console.warn(`[mcp-pool] could not load ${settingsPath}:`, (err as Error).message);\\n    // Fallback: bridge-owned servers only.\\n    return {\\n      memory: { command: \\\"node\\\", args: [path.resolve(\\\"dist/mcp-memory.js\\\")] },\\n      google: { command: \\\"node\\\", args: [path.resolve(\\\"dist/mcp-google.js\\\")] },\\n    };\\n  }\\n}\\n\",\"structuredPatch\":[{\"oldStart\":53,\"oldLines\":7,\"newStart\":53,\"newLines\":9,\"lines\":[\"     const transport = new StdioClientTransport({\",\"       command: cfg.command,\",\"       args: cfg.args ?? [],\",\"-      env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\",\"+      // TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN 不隨 process.env 外洩給 MCP 子行程（同 acpClient 的\",\"+      // buildSpawnEnv）；置於 cfg.env 之前——設定檔明確給值時仍可覆蓋。\",\"+      env: { ...process.env, TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN: \\\"\\\", ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv } as Record<string, string>,\",\"     });\",\"     const client = new Client({ name: `bridge-pool-${name}`, version: \\\"1.0.0\\\" });\",\"     await client.connect(transport);\"]}],\"userModified\":false,\"replaceAll\":false}"</outcome>
</observed_from_primary_session>

content.thinking:
Great! Now I see the actual fix being implemented. The primary session edited mcp-pool.ts to explicitly set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN to an empty string before spreading cfg.env and extraEnv. This prevents the token from leaking to MCP child processes while still allowing it to be explicitly overridden if specified in the config.

The change is:
- Before: `env: { ...process.env, ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv }`
- After: `env: { ...process.env, TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN: "", ...cfg.env, ...extraEnv }`

With a comment explaining that TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is not leaked to MCP child processes (same as acpClient's buildSpawnEnv), and it's placed before cfg.env so config files can still explicitly override if needed.

This is a security bugfix. Let me create an observation.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Prevent TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN leakage to MCP child processes</title>
  <subtitle>mcp-pool.ts now explicitly blanks TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN before spawning MCP servers to prevent environment variable leakage</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>mcp-pool.ts connectOne method modified to set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN to empty string in child process environment</fact>
    <fact>Token blank occurs after spreading process.env but before cfg.env and extraEnv, allowing explicit config override</fact>
    <fact>Pattern mirrors acpClient's buildSpawnEnv approach for consistent security posture</fact>
    <fact>Fix prevents sensitive bot credentials from propagating to MCP server child processes</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed environment variable leakage vulnerability in mcp-pool.ts where TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN was being passed to MCP server child processes through process.env spread. The connectOne method now explicitly sets TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN to an empty string after spreading process.env but before applying cfg.env and extraEnv overrides. This ensures the sensitive bot token does not leak to child processes by default, while still allowing configuration files to explicitly provide it if needed. The implementation follows the same pattern used in acpClient's buildSpawnEnv for consistency across the codebase.</narrative>

    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>

</observation>

content.thinking:
The work has been committed. The fix prevents TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN from leaking to MCP child processes by explicitly blanking it after spreading process.env but before cfg.env/extraEnv. The commit message explains:
- This follows the same defense pattern as acpClient.buildSpawnEnv
- The blank is positioned before cfg.env so explicit config values can still override (respecting intentional behavior)
- No MCP servers or callers in the repo depend on this token (the only call chain direct.ts:85 ← sessionManager.ts:342 only passes MEMORY_DIR)
- Commit hash: 33c261c

I should update my observation to reflect that this work is now committed and deployed.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>MCP child processes no longer inherit TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN</title>
  <subtitle>Fixed environment variable leakage by explicitly blanking bot token in mcp-pool.ts before spawning MCP servers</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>mcp-pool.ts connectOne method now sets TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN to empty string after spreading process.env</fact>
    <fact>Token blank positioned before cfg.env and extraEnv to allow explicit configuration override if intentionally specified</fact>
    <fact>Implementation mirrors acpClient.buildSpawnEnv defense pattern for consistency</fact>
    <fact>No MCP servers or callers in repository depend on TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN; only MEMORY_DIR is passed through the call chain</fact>
    <fact>Committed as 33c261c to main branch</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Reduced attack surface by preventing TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN from leaking to MCP server child processes. The mcp-pool.ts connectOne method previously spread all process.env variables to child processes, exposing sensitive credentials. Now explicitly blanks the bot token after spreading process.env but before applying cfg.env and extraEnv overrides. This positioning allows configuration files to explicitly provide the token if needed (respecting intentional behavior) while blocking unintended inheritance. The pattern matches acpClient.buildSpawnEnv for consistent security posture across the codebase. Analysis confirmed no MCP servers or calling code depend on this token - the only call chain (direct.ts line 85 called from sessionManager.ts line 342) only passes MEMORY_DIR through mcpEnv.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/provider/mcp-pool.ts</file>
    <file>src/provider/direct.ts</file>
    <file>src/sessionManager.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/provider/mcp-pool.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
